text: In the last few decades, almost all European countries with conscript forces have switched to either voluntary or contract-based all-volunteer forces. However, new challenges facing European security have sparked conversations on conscription in some countries. At the same time, modern conscript forces are operating in an increasingly individualistic and neoliberal society. This article examines different forms of conscription and its contemporary relevance and vitality in the context of six European countries: Estonia, Finland, Lithuania, Norway, Sweden and Switzerland. While four of the countries have maintained conscription, and continue to do so, Lithuania and Sweden reintroduced conscription recently. The conscription systems in the six countries diverge, and the dominant focus on conscript motivation and women in the military is evolving. The core characteristics that describe conscript forces include three elements: the conscription rate (i.e. the number of conscripts as a proportion of each birth cohort), conscript motivation and the way women are incorporated into conscript forces. By combining the characteristics of conscription systems, we can classify them into three categories, and argue more generally that conscription is a dynamic institution that has adapted to new societal demands and to changing circumstances, rather than standing still at the traditional status quo.
